   - Goal: recipients of older client calls that expect X-field continue to receive best-effort responses while the control plane serves both vX and vY schemas. Shim should be opt-in via enable_runtime_shim=true and shim_mode=passive.
   - Implementation notes: shim is an inline adapter in the API gateway that translates deprecated response fields into v2 shapes (eg. "prefix_cache_version" -> "cache_meta.version") and forwards unknowns downstream. Keep latency + P50 add <2ms.
   - Smoke checks: synthetic requests from both old and new SDKs return 200 and validate schema with jsonschema.

4) Run idempotent KV/prefix backfill job (non-blocking)
   - Goal: seed new namespace keys for new runtime format while leaving existing keys intact.
   - Approach: consumer-driven backfill: a job iterates active keys and writes new-format shadow keys prefixed with "shadow_v2:". Write is conditional (only if not exists).
   - Rate control: use kv_backfill_batch_size and backfill_rate_per_min to avoid bursting.
   - Observables: backfill progress logs, 'shadow keys created' gauge, store latency.
   - Rollback: safe - shadow keys can be deleted; if job misbehaves, stop job, leave shadow keys intact until investigation.

5) Read-path experiment (read-by-preference)
   - Goal: read from new shadow_v2 key if present, else fallback to old key. This is done by enabling shim_mode=active but still writing to old and shadow keys.
   - How: incremental config per shard/region. Start with 1% of shards. Use canary routing based on shard id.
   - Observables: per-shard error rate, response divergence metric (percent of requests that returned data from shadow vs old), latency P95.
   - Rollback trigger: if divergence > 1% of expected or latency P95 increases > 20% for more than 5min, revert preference to old keys.

6) Promote write-path (write dual, then switch)
   - Step A: convert writing logic to write both old and shadow_v2 keys for all traffic (dual-write).
   - Step B: after N hours of stability, switch read preference to shadow_v2 globally, then retire old keys after observation window.
   - Observables: write success rate, size of replicas, storage pressure.
   - Rollback: flip write-path back to write-old-only, stop new writes, continue serving from old.

7) Cleanup and remove shim
   - Goal: remove shim and shadow keys once all clients are confirmed upgraded.
   - Action: run a TTL-sweep for shadow keys, remove terraform vars by flipping enable_runtime_shim=false and removing code paths in later change.

Terraform notes / module concerns:
- Variables must default to a safe state (disabled). Do not add resource-level changes that touch state in the same PR as new vars.
- Add a small local-exec or null_resource hooks for backfill orchestration if customers want to run the job as part of apply. Prefer separate CI job.
- Use feature flags at the service-module boundary (not across global infra) so customers with multiple clusters can run different shard-level flags.
- Keep a documented mapping of tf var -> runtime behavior in the module README.

Smoke tests and verification suite (what to run per checkpoint):
- Schema validation tests (old sdk + new sdk) against gateway endpoints.
- Latency regression synthetic test (loadgen with 95th percentile threshold).
- KV consistency check: sample N keys randomly and compare old vs shadow values.
- Canary traffic report: shard-level success/failure and latency deltas.

Rollback playbook (fast paths):
- If API contract errors spike: flip gateway shim_mode to passive, or disable enable_runtime_shim at TF level (fast rollback).
- If latency or error rates increase in canary shards: revert read-preference and abort promotion steps, leave shadow keys in place for later repair.
- If backfill job miswrote keys (data corruption): stop job, snapshot kv store (if supported) and run corrective script to delete shadow keys, restore from snapshot if needed.

Known risks and mitigations:
- Storage blowup: dual writes double storage temporarily. Mitigate by monitoring store free space and using conservative backfill rate.
- Edge agent incompatibility: older agents may ignore new fields; shims should be defensive. Keep a list of agent versions that are known incompatible and schedule them for upgrade.
- Race conditions on conditional writes: prefer server-side conditional write (if-not-exists) to avoid overwrites across shards.

Open questions / TODOs:
- Decide TTL for shadow keys before we garbage collect (30d suggested, needs product input).
- Add an automated TF plan validator that ensures enable_runtime_shim default=false in customer modules.
- Decide who owns the backfill job (SE-run vs automated pipeline).

Rough timeline (example for a 4-week window):
- Week 0: feature-flag TF + shim prototype in staging.
- Week 1: run small backfill for low-traffic region.
- Week 2: expand read-preference to 10% -> 30% if stable.
- Week 3: promote dual-write and global read-switch.
- Week 4: monitor and cleanup shadow keys after TTL.

Notes to self / meeting pickup points:
- Coordinate with SRE on alert thresholds (P95 + error %).
- Get signoff from product for shadow TTL.
- Add a short-runbook snippet to the on-call handbook for quick disable of shims.

Appendix: quick commands and checks (placeholders)
- List active agent versions: kubectl -n redwood get pods -o custom-columns=VERSION:.metadata.labels.agent_version | sort | uniq -c
- Backfill progress: curl -s https://backfill.local/metrics | jq '.backfill{status}
- Canary traffic report: dashboards/redwood_private/canary-summary

End.

title: stability-handles-routing-interop-notes
preview:
Purpose:
Quick exploratory notes on how model stability guarantees (what we call 'stability handles') should interact with routing policies and the control plane across multi-region deployments. Trying to capture a compact, implementable set of behaviors rather than another academic matrix.

Summary / TL;DR:
- Introduce two orthogonal primitives: "soft-handle" (best-effort version affinity) and "hard-handle" (strict version binding with strong rollout semantics).
- Control plane must expose deterministic resolution that routers can use with minimal latency (cache-friendly).
- Compatibility checks happen in two phases: cheap stateless compatibility probe at routing time, and full compatibility verification at commit time when a registry change is applied globally.
- Fallback strategy defined per-handle: fall back to regional compatible variant -> autoscaled generic variant -> failover to hosted fallback model.

Glossary (informal):
- stability handle: a customer-specified expectation on model version behavior (soft/hard).
- soft-handle: route preference; system can move traffic if needed for SLOs/capacity.
- hard-handle: strong guarantee; control plane should avoid swapping away unless explicit operator action or emergency rollback.
- compatibility fingerprint: small version-identifier + ABI-ish flags used for fast comparisons.

Goals for this doc:
- Produce a simple state machine that can be implemented in the control plane and cached at edge routers.
- Minimize coordination during per-request routing decision.
- Define observability events to detect silent substitutions.
- Provide rollback rules and a safety valve for cross-region inconsistencies.

Constraints / Real-world considerations:
- Edge routers must not call control plane synchronously per request. Need a TTLed cache (e.g., 10s-60s) and a small compatibility fingerprint for fast local checks.
- Registry commits are eventually-consistent across regions; we cannot assume linearizable global state unless we pay the latency.
- Customers expect either soft semantics (lower cost, higher availability) or hard semantics (predictability for legal/compliance).
- Model variants (quantized/narrow-context) may be ABI-compatible for inference but produce statistically different outputs — must be visible in compatibility metadata.

Design sketch:
1) Metadata model for registry entries (small, cacheable):
   - model_id: string
   - version_id: string
   - fingerprint: short hex (e.g., 8 chars) -> includes arch/kv-cache-layout/IO-format
   - compatibility_tags: list (e.g., ["struct:chat","tok:cl100k","kv:v1","gen-v2"])
   - stability_handle: "soft" | "hard"
   - fallback_order: list of model_ids (minimally sized)

2) Two-phase compatibility checks:
   Phase A — Routing-time fast check (stateless):
     - Router has cached metadata for (model_id, pinned_version_fingerprint).
     - If incoming request demands a particular stability_handle/hard binding, router compares request fingerprint to cached fingerprint. If match -> route.
     - If mismatch: consult small precomputed compatibility bitmap cached with routing table (bitmap says index of compatible local variant). If compatible -> route to compatible variant.
     - If no compatible local variant -> consult fallback_order (cache) -> pick first available replica.
     - If nothing available -> trigger fallback telemetry + return fallback model or 503 depending on customer's preference.

   Phase B — Commit-time verification (control plane):
     - When registry update (new version, change stability_handle, change fallback_order) is proposed, the control plane runs a full compatibility verification workflow (automated tests, evals, tokenization checks).
     - Only when verification passes, control plane publishes a new fingerprint and an epoch id.
     - Publication is replicated async across regions; routers reference (model_id, epoch) pairs.

3) Epoched cache and monotonicity guarantees:
   - Routers track epoch numbers per model_id. If router receives request bound to epoch E but its local epoch < E, it can either:
     a) use last-known compatible variant and mark request with 'stale-metadata' tag in trace, or
     b) block up to N ms to fetch updated metadata (dangerous for latency SLOs).
   - Default: do (a) and emit high-priority telemetry. This prefers availability.

Compatibility scoring heuristic (for commit-time):
- Score = base - (tokenization_delta_weight * tokenization_delta) - (kv_layout_mismatch * 50) - (quant_diff_penalty) - (eval_quality_drop * 100)
- We should keep a tiny compatibility table that reduces to 3 buckets: compatible, degraded-but-acceptable, incompatible. Routers only need the bucket, not the full score.

Example decision table (router-local view):
Request: customer requests model A@epoch 42 with hard-handle
Router cache: A@epoch 42 -> fingerprint f42, local replica: A@f42 present -> route OK
Router cache: A@epoch 42 -> fingerprint f42, local replica: A@f41 present -> f41 vs f42 differ in kv-layout -> if handle==hard -> do not route, consult fallback_order -> fall back

Fallback hierarchy sketch (per-request):
1) same-version replica in same AZ/region
2) compatible-version replica in same region
3) dedicated generic inference pool for customer (best-effort)
4) hosted low-latency fallback model (explicit opt-in)
5) fail (503) -> only when customer disallows fallback

Failure modes to watch for:
- Split-brain publish: two regions publish different epochs for same model_id (operator error).
  - Detection: monotonicity checks on model epoch stream + cross-region checksum comparison.
  - Mitigation: freeze routing for that model until operator resolves; default to oldest stable epoch.
- Silent substitute: router uses a compatible variant but customer expects exact version.
  - Detection: traces with version fingerprint on every response and scoreboard alert if mismatch frequency climbs.
- Roll-forward race: control plane publishes epoch N, routers see it at different times -> transient inconsistent routing.
  - Mitigation: publish 'grace window' metadata: routers should not treat new epoch as authoritative until timestamp T + delta.

Observability / telemetry (must-haves):
- Per-request: requested_model, served_model_fingerprint, epoch_seen_by_router, route_decision_reason (hit/mismatch/fallback)
- Control plane events: commit_id, verification_status, eval_metrics_summary (brief), regions_replicated_count
- Alerts: sudden jump in 'serving-fingerprint != requested-fingerprint' by >0.5% over baseline for 5min

Rollout + rollback playbook (high level):
- Canary: publish new epoch with stability_handle=soft and targeted to 2% traffic -> monitor eval metrics and customer-facing regressions for 24h -> escalate to 20%/50%/100% if good.
- For hard-handles: require manual approval and a small-stage rollout (unless operator explicitly forces global).
- Automatic rollback triggers: eval-quality-drop > threshold OR customer rejection flag hits -> control plane retract epoch (publish a retraction message + ring fence new epoch).

Open questions / tradeoffs (TODOs):
- Should routers support making fallback decisions based on live load metrics or purely static cached fallback_order? (tradeoff: correctness vs latency)
- What is the appropriate TTL range for router caches? 5s, 30s, 60s? Longer TTLs reduce control-plane load but increase stale behavior.
- Do we need a separate "legal/stability" knob for enterprise customers that really want a synchronous commit confirmation on version switch? (probably yes, but opt-in expensive)
- Implementation detail: how to encode compatibility bitmap to keep packets small? protobuf with delta compression?

Immediate action items:
- Liam: prototype small fingerprint format RFC (1 pager) -> due 2024-11-01
- Evelyn: sketch commit-time verification checklist (automated tests we can run in CI) -> due 2024-11-07
- Priya: implement trace key proposal and dashboards for 'requested_vs_served' metric (POC) -> due 2024-11-15

Notes-to-self:
- Keep this doc intentionally terse; next step is a 'contract' YAML that the control plane publishes and routers consume.
- Important to separate semantics (soft/hard) from enforcement mechanism (cache/epoch/fallback).

References and similar work (internal):
- PLAT-242 internal notes on registry snapshots
- SRE runbooks for cross-region replication (look up in /shared_drives/engineering/site-reliability-engineering)
- Prior discussion thread: slack #platform-routing 2024-09-12 (search "epoched registry")

End of note.

title: Concord upgrade orchestration notes
preview:
Overview:

This is a working notes doc capturing the proposed orchestration and safety techniques for the next Control Plane + Runtime upgrade (internal codename: Concord). The focus here is staged interop across existing deployed customers (VPC and on-prem appliances) with minimal operator action, deterministic rollback triggers, and Terraform flagging strategy so Installers can run with confidence.

Goals:
- upgrade control-plane API from v1beta3 -> v1 (breaking changes limited to feature flags only)
- allow mixed-version runtime nodes (legacy runtime 2.x and concord runtime 3.x) to coexist for at least 48 hours per customer
- zero-downtime for inference paths with automated fallback to pre-upgrade model variant when compatibility risk detected
- automated rollback based on telemetry thresholds (latency, error-rate, token-drop)

Non-Goals:
- Mass database schema migration that requires offline window (we will instead ship schema compatibility layer and background migration).

High-level approach:
1) Preflight stage: server-side feature gating via Terraform variables + local verification scripts.
2) Canary stage: route 1-5% of traffic to Concord control plane + new runtime pool with strict telemetry gates.
3) Gradual rollout: increase to 25% then 60% then 100% with pause windows and automatic rollback if gates fail.
4) Post-rollout reconciliation: background schema migration and KV compression tasks on a rolling basis.
5) Clean-up: deprecation of shims after 30 days if no regressions.

Preflight checklist (operator):
- Ensure customer has at least 2 dedicated control plane replicas in VPC, and runtime pool has >= 20% spare capacity for canary traffic.
- Run `concord preflight-check --env <customer>` from operator workstation. Expected output: PASS for network, PASS for disk, WARN for tf-flag mismatch if present.
- Validate Terraform flag file in repo: `infra/customer-x/flags.tfvars`. Required keys: `concord_enabled = false` (default), `concord_canary_capacity = 0`, `control_plane_api_version = "v1beta3"`.
- Create staging resources if customer requested ephemeral test namespace: `tf apply -var-file=staging-namespace.tfvars`.

Terraform module notes:
- Introduce `module "concord_controlplane"` with inputs: `api_version`, `compatibility_policy` (values: permissive|strict|shim), `canary_capacity_pct` integer.
- Modules must be backwards compatible: if `api_version` omitted, default to current stable `v1beta3`.
- Add lifecycle rule `create_before_destroy = true` on control-plane ELB and DNS alias resources to avoid DNS flap.
- Example override snippet for ops: 
  variable "concord_enabled" {
    type = bool
    default = false
  }

  module "controlplane" {
    source = "git::https://github.com/redwood-inference/terraform-modules.git//controlplane?ref=main"
    api_version = var.concord_enabled ? "v1" : "v1beta3"
    canary_capacity_pct = var.concord_canary_capacity
  }

Compatibility gating strategy:
- API shim layer: control-plane will accept both v1 and v1beta3 payloads and translate internally for a 30-day window. This avoids immediate client breakage.
- Runtime handshake: new runtime advertises `runtime_version: 3.x` and supports legacy KV format. If the control-plane sees a 3.x node it will mark node as `compat: mixed`; routing will avoid sending large batch inference jobs (>16k tokens) to mixed nodes during canary.
- Model artifacts: new runtime may require quantized weights in a different layout. During canary, we register model variants with `compat: legacy` and `compat: concord` tags and the platform will choose compatible artifacts per node.

Canary orchestration details:
- Traffic shaping: use Smart Routing to send percentage by route tag. Canary schedule: 1% -> 5% (2h) -> 5% stability check (6h) -> 25% (12h) -> 60% (24h) -> 100% (48h).
- Telemetry gates at each increase:
  - P90 latency regression <= +20% relative to baseline
  - Error rate increase <= +0.5% absolute
  - Token dropout events: 0 allowed in first 24h, then <= 0.1%
- If any gate fails, automated rollback to previous control plane version and runtime pool for that customer.

Automated rollback triggers and sequence:
1) Gate evaluation job `upgrade-gate-evaluator` runs every 5 minutes against customer telemetry. If gate fails consecutively 3 out of 4 checks, raise `upgrade-alert`.
2) Alert auto-creates rollback ticket (JIRA-DEP-xxxx) and invokes `concord rollback --customer <id> --reason <metrics>` which will:
   a) Set TF var `concord_canary_capacity=0` and `control_plane_api_version=v1beta3` and apply with `-auto-approve`.
   b) Drain 3.x runtime nodes with `kubectl cordon` and move them to quarantine pool.
   c) Reconfigure Smart Routing to prefer pinned pre-upgrade model variants.
3) Post-rollback health check runs for 1 hour; if stable, close the ticket.

State migration approach (DB + KV):
- Avoid synchronous schema upgrade. Add compatibility columns and background migrator `kv-reconcile` that processes items at low QPS.
- For critical indices we will do a dual-write during canary: new control-plane writes to both legacy and new tables for a configurable period.
- Example migration policy:
  - Day 0-2: dual-write enabled, reads favor legacy index.
  - Day 3-14: gradual read switch to new index for low-risk routes only.
  - Day 15+: full reads from new index, legacy reads deprecated but retained for 30 days for emergency backout.

Observability and SLOs to monitor during upgrade:
- Baseline capture: 72h pre-upgrade baseline capture for latency percentiles, error rates, GPU utilization, kv-latency.
- Add rollout metrics dashboard `dash/Concord-Rollout-<customer>` with alert thresholds mirrored to PagerDuty escalation policy `PD-UPGRADE-OPS`.
- Instrumentation hooks: add `upgrade_phase` tag to all traces being routed via new control-plane so we can filter spans.

Testing matrix (minimal):
- Unit: API contract tests for control-plane compatibility (mock both v1 and v1beta3 payloads).
- Integration: spin up single-customer full stack in CI with `--with-db-migration` flag. CI job name: `concord-int-helm-quick`
- Chaos: run node-restart chaos during 5% canary window to validate reconnection semantics.
- Performance: sample endpoint stress test with QA workload, assert latency/SLA.

Operator commands and scripts (working):
- preflight: `python ops/concord/preflight.py --customer=acme --tf=infra/acme/flags.tfvars`
- begin canary: `concord rollout start --customer acme --canary 5`
- force rollback: `concord rollback --customer acme --immediate --reason "p90 spike"`
- view gate status: `concord gates status --customer acme`

Known risks and mitigations:
- Risk: TF drift between ops-run and customer-managed infra.
  Mitigation: add `concord_managed = true|false` flag and detect mismatch at preflight with `tf plan` output parsing.
- Risk: Model artifact mismatch leading to runtime OOM.
  Mitigation: register artifact metadata and enforce `artifact_compat_check` in registry; fail canary if mismatches found.
- Risk: DNS TTL causes stale routing after rollback.
  Mitigation: use ephemeral DNS alias and ensure `create_before_destroy` on resources + low TTL during rollout windows.

Post-upgrade clean-up and follow-ups:
- Schedule background job to disable shims after 30 days and notify customers via support pipeline.
- Run reconciliation job `kv-reconcile --customer acme --throttle=0.5` to finish migrations.
- Capture postmortem on any rollback and tag with `upgrade-retro-<customer>-YYYYMMDD` in Confluence.

Open TODOs (next steps in doc):
- Add explicit Terraform code example for module `concord_controlplane` to library repo.
- Lockdown automatic rollback scope: define whether rollback should include DB schema revert or only control plane + runtime. Current plan: rollback excludes DB revert (safe-first).
- Add backwards-compat tests for third-party model loaders used by customers.

Notes and links:
- Related PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/orchestration/pull/786
- Example rollback ticket: JIRA-DEP-1245
- Slack channel: #private-deploy-upgrades thread FG4x9

End of working notes.

-- Priya

- Mass database schema migration that requires offline window (we will instead ship schema compatibility layer and background migration).

High-level approach:
1) Preflight stage: server-side feature gating via Terraform variables + local verification scripts.
2) Canary stage: route 1-5% of traffic to Concord control plane + new runtime pool with strict telemetry gates.
3) Gradual rollout: increase to 25% then 60% then 100% with pause windows and automatic rollback if gates fail.
4) Post-rollout reconciliation: background schema migration and KV compression tasks on a rolling basis.
5) Clean-up: deprecation of shims after 30 days if no regressions.

Preflight checklist (operator):
- Ensure customer has at least 2 dedicated control plane replicas in VPC, and runtime pool has >= 20% spare capacity for canary traffic.
- Run `concord preflight-check --env <customer>` from operator workstation. Expected output: PASS for network, PASS for disk, WARN for tf-flag mismatch if present.
- Validate Terraform flag file in repo: `infra/customer-x/flags.tfvars`. Required keys: `concord_enabled = false` (default), `concord_canary_capacity = 0`, `control_plane_api_version = "v1beta3"`.
- Create staging resources if customer requested ephemeral test namespace: `tf apply -var-file=staging-namespace.tfvars`.

Terraform module notes:
- Introduce `module "concord_controlplane"` with inputs: `api_version`, `compatibility_policy` (values: permissive|strict|shim), `canary_capacity_pct` integer.
- Modules must be backwards compatible: if `api_version` omitted, default to current stable `v1beta3`.
- Add lifecycle rule `create_before_destroy = true` on control-plane ELB and DNS alias resources to avoid DNS flap.
- Example override snippet for ops: 
  variable "concord_enabled" {
    type = bool
    default = false
  }

  module "controlplane" {
    source = "git::https://github.com/redwood-inference/terraform-modules.git//controlplane?ref=main"
    api_version = var.concord_enabled ? "v1" : "v1beta3"
    canary_capacity_pct = var.concord_canary_capacity
  }

Compatibility gating strategy:
- API shim layer: control-plane will accept both v1 and v1beta3 payloads and translate internally for a 30-day window. This avoids immediate client breakage.
- Runtime handshake: new runtime advertises `runtime_version: 3.x` and supports legacy KV format. If the control-plane sees a 3.x node it will mark node as `compat: mixed`; routing will avoid sending large batch inference jobs (>16k tokens) to mixed nodes during canary.
- Model artifacts: new runtime may require quantized weights in a different layout. During canary, we register model variants with `compat: legacy` and `compat: concord` tags and the platform will choose compatible artifacts per node.

Canary orchestration details:
- Traffic shaping: use Smart Routing to send percentage by route tag. Canary schedule: 1% -> 5% (2h) -> 5% stability check (6h) -> 25% (12h) -> 60% (24h) -> 100% (48h).
- Telemetry gates at each increase:
  - P90 latency regression <= +20% relative to baseline
  - Error rate increase <= +0.5% absolute
  - Token dropout events: 0 allowed in first 24h, then <= 0.1%
- If any gate fails, automated rollback to previous control plane version and runtime pool for that customer.

Automated rollback triggers and sequence:
1) Gate evaluation job `upgrade-gate-evaluator` runs every 5 minutes against customer telemetry. If gate fails consecutively 3 out of 4 checks, raise `upgrade-alert`.
2) Alert auto-creates rollback ticket (JIRA-DEP-xxxx) and invokes `concord rollback --customer <id> --reason <metrics>` which will:
   a) Set TF var `concord_canary_capacity=0` and `control_plane_api_version=v1beta3` and apply with `-auto-approve`.
   b) Drain 3.x runtime nodes with `kubectl cordon` and move them to quarantine pool.
   c) Reconfigure Smart Routing to prefer pinned pre-upgrade model variants.
3) Post-rollback health check runs for 1 hour; if stable, close the ticket.

State migration approach (DB + KV):
- Avoid synchronous schema upgrade. Add compatibility columns and background migrator `kv-reconcile` that processes items at low QPS.
- For critical indices we will do a dual-write during canary: new control-plane writes to both legacy and new tables for a configurable period.
- Example migration policy:
  - Day 0-2: dual-write enabled, reads favor legacy index.
  - Day 3-14: gradual read switch to new index for low-risk routes only.
  - Day 15+: full reads from new index, legacy reads deprecated but retained for 30 days for emergency backout.

Observability and SLOs to monitor during upgrade:
- Baseline capture: 72h pre-upgrade baseline capture for latency percentiles, error rates, GPU utilization, kv-latency.
- Add rollout metrics dashboard `dash/Concord-Rollout-<customer>` with alert thresholds mirrored to PagerDuty escalation policy `PD-UPGRADE-OPS`.
- Instrumentation hooks: add `upgrade_phase` tag to all traces being routed via new control-plane so we can filter spans.

Testing matrix (minimal):
- Unit: API contract tests for control-plane compatibility (mock both v1 and v1beta3 payloads).
- Integration: spin up single-customer full stack in CI with `--with-db-migration` flag. CI job name: `concord-int-helm-quick`
- Chaos: run node-restart chaos during 5% canary window to validate reconnection semantics.
- Performance: sample endpoint stress test with QA workload, assert latency/SLA.

Operator commands and scripts (working):
- preflight: `python ops/concord/preflight.py --customer=acme --tf=infra/acme/flags.tfvars`
- begin canary: `concord rollout start --customer acme --canary 5`
- force rollback: `concord rollback --customer acme --immediate --reason "p90 spike"`
- view gate status: `concord gates status --customer acme`

Known risks and mitigations:
- Risk: TF drift between ops-run and customer-managed infra.
  Mitigation: add `concord_managed = true|false` flag and detect mismatch at preflight with `tf plan` output parsing.
- Risk: Model artifact mismatch leading to runtime OOM.
  Mitigation: register artifact metadata and enforce `artifact_compat_check` in registry; fail canary if mismatches found.
- Risk: DNS TTL causes stale routing after rollback.
  Mitigation: use ephemeral DNS alias and ensure `create_before_destroy` on resources + low TTL during rollout windows.

Post-upgrade clean-up and follow-ups:
- Schedule background job to disable shims after 30 days and notify customers via support pipeline.
- Run reconciliation job `kv-reconcile --customer acme --throttle=0.5` to finish migrations.
- Capture postmortem on any rollback and tag with `upgrade-retro-<customer>-YYYYMMDD` in Confluence.

Open TODOs (next steps in doc):
- Add explicit Terraform code example for module `concord_controlplane` to library repo.
- Lockdown automatic rollback scope: define whether rollback should include DB schema revert or only control plane + runtime. Current plan: rollback excludes DB revert (safe-first).
- Add backwards-compat tests for third-party model loaders used by customers.

Notes and links:
- Related PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/orchestration/pull/786
- Example rollback ticket: JIRA-DEP-1245
- Slack channel: #private-deploy-upgrades thread FG4x9

End of working notes.

-- Priya

title: Preflight check engine for private deployments — draft notes
preview:
Purpose:
Draft notes for a lightweight preflight validation engine that sits between CI and GitOps/Argo for private (VPC / on-prem) deployments. The goal: fail fast on bad artifacts, infra drift, or unsafe config before we create Argo Applications or run Helm upgrades in customer VPCs/on-prem clusters.

Scope (what this doc covers):
- Artifact-level validations: image signature, SBOM, provenance, tag immutability
- Infra assertions: Terraform plan checks, required outputs present, KMS + network readiness
- Helm/chart validation: helm lint + template render with values + schema checks
- Environment sanity: node selectors, GPU quotas, disk, DNS, LB, storageClass existence
- Gating policies: who can override, emergency bypass, attestation records retention
- Integration points: CI (GH Actions/GitLab), Terraform CD (apply hooks), Argo CD Application creation, SRE alerting + automated rollback triggers

High-level flow (sequence):
1) PR opens against helm-values / chart / tf-modules repo. CI runs unit checks.
2) CI builds image, publishes to private registry, signs artifact and generates SBOM.
3) Preflight job triggers (either as part of CI or as a separate pipeline):
   - fetch signed artifact metadata (signature, signer id, sbom link)
   - run artifact validation (signature verify, SBOM policy, vulnerability threshold)
   - terraform plan assertions (use plan file or plan-json to run module-level checks)
   - helm template + policy checks (kubeval, schema validation, custom policies)
   - environment prechecks (reachability to cluster api, required nodes, storage class)
4) If all checks pass, the pipeline commits a deployment manifest (or triggers Argo Application creation) with an attestation record (signed by CI key) attached.
5) Argo sync uses the manifest; admission controllers in the cluster enforce runtime policies (e.g., image signature verification via Cosign/Kubernetes admission webhook).
6) Canary / progressive rollout controlled by Argo Rollouts/ApplicationSet; metrics-driven promotion or automatic rollback.

Preflight checks: concrete checklist (implementable)
- Artifact provenance:
  - Verify container image signature with Cosign (CI key or delegated signer): exit non-zero if invalid.
  - Confirm SBOM present and accessible. Extract package list and run vuln scan (Trivy/Grype).
  - Check image tag policy: forbid floating tags on private-deployments (no :latest).
  - Confirm image digest pin recorded in manifest (image@sha256:...).

- SBOM / Vulnerability policy:
  - Block if critical CVEs present in SBOM or if new CVE count increased by >X vs baseline.
  - Optional break-glass: security team approval required for exceptions (recorded in ticket).

- Terraform infra assertions:
  - Pull terraform plan json (tf plan -out=plan && terraform show -json plan).
  - Validate that required outputs are present (e.g., cluster_id, bastion_ip, kms_key_arn).
  - Assert network ranges do not overlap with protected CIDRs (customer-specified constraints).
  - Confirm provider plugin versions pinned in module to avoid drift.
  - Check plan for destructive operations on production KMS keys or storage volumes; require manual approval.

- Helm/Manifest validation:
  - helm lint (helm v3).
  - helm template --values=... and run kubeval + open-policy-agent checks (Rego policies for our constraints).
  - Validate resource requests/limits present for all containers.
  - Confirm PodSecurityContext / securityContext meet baseline.
  - Ensure Service type, ingress hostnames align with VPC DNS plan.

- Environment live checks:
  - API reachability: kubectl --context=target get ns (fail early)
  - Node labels and taints: check for required nodePools and capacities (GPUs, memory).
  - Storage classes exist and dynamic provisioners available.
  - CNI health: needed for cross-namespace traffic; quick curl against dns svc to verify forwarding.

- Policy & attestation:
  - On success, produce a signed attestation envelope (using CI short-lived key) containing: artifact digest, sbom link, tf-plan-hash, helm-chart-hash, preflight-checksum, timestamp, job-id.
  - Store attestation in central artifact store (S3/minio) and add reference to deployment manifest (annotation).
  - Maintain audit log: who triggered pipeline, which commit, approvers.

Terraform module notes (practical patterns):
- Expose lightweight preflight outputs from TF modules (e.g., expected node pool names, minimum node counts, required storage classes, allowed CIDRs) so pipeline can assert environment readiness without full apply.
- Add a tf module flag 'prevent_destroy' default true for critical resources; make plan check fail if plan contains destroy=true for those resources unless PR includes explicit override and approval.
- Use separate state/environments for preflight plan vs apply; preflight should use the same backend but read-only plan artifacts.
- Store a small metadata file in the module release (module-version.json) that contains provider versions and known-good flags. CI preflight compares local module usage against known-good.

Upgrade / rollout plan integration points:
- CI produces a release bundle: signed image digest, rendered helm templates (for auditing), attestation envelope, tf-plan-hash.
- Argo Application manifests annotated with attestation reference. Argo cannot sync unless cluster admission validates attestation (webhook).
- Canary strategy: route X% traffic to new deployment; monitor these metrics at the edge and application level: p50/p95 latency, error rate, token spend (for LLM endpoints), QPS, memory/gc behavior.
- Promotion criteria: all metrics within SLO bounds for Y minutes and no new critical errors reported by logging alerts.
- Auto-rollback: if error rate > Z% for 5 minutes or latency regresses > 2x baseline, trigger Argo Rollout abort and revert to previous image via a pinned digest.

CI job sketch (GitHub Actions style):
- jobs.preflight: runs on PR or main->release tag. Steps:
  - checkout, set env
  - build-image: docker build ... && docker push (only on release branch)
  - sign-image: cosign sign --key ci-key ... (store signature in artifact store)
  - generate-sbom: syft packages ... && upload sbom.json
  - artifact-validate: cosign verify && trivy sbom scan --ignore-unfixed --exit-code 1 on policy fail
  - tf-plan: terraform init && terraform plan -out=plan && terraform show -json plan > plan.json
  - tf-assert: run ./tools/tf-asserts.py --plan plan.json --checks required_outputs,prevent_destroy
  - helm-validate: helm lint && helm template | kubeval && opa eval --input - --data policy.rego
  - env-probe: run ./tools/cluster-probes.sh --context staging-tenant-A
  - produce-attestation: ./tools/sign-attestation --payload metadata.json --key ci-key
  - post-status: comment on PR with attestation id + link to artifacts

Failure handling and human workflows:
- If preflight fails due to security findings, create SECURITY exception ticket (link in PR) and notify sec-team channel.
- If preflight fails due to infra plan changes (unexpected destroy), block merge and require infra-owner approval (Jira approval ticket).
- For transient environment probes (network flakiness), retry policy: 3 attempts with backoff; if still failing, create infra alert for SRE to investigate.

Runtime enforcement (cluster-side):
- Image attestation webhook: rejects images without attestation references or untrusted signers.
- OPA admission: denies resources missing required labels/annotations (e.g., owner, env, ticket id).
- Pod security admission + PSP replacement: enforce baseline controls.

Storage and KMS considerations:
- Attestations stored encrypted in artifact bucket; use tenant-specific KMS keys for private deploys.
- Key rotation: CI signs with ephemeral key delegated by central KMS; rotation should maintain verification capabilities by publishing public keys to a keystore and recording key id in attestation.

Observability + metrics to surface in preflight results dashboard:
- Preflight run duration, failure rate by check type, time to remediation, top failing PRs, top offending CVEs, tf-plan destructive ops count.
- Map preflight failures to billing/ops impact (approx. time saved by preventing a failed deploy).

Open questions / tradeoffs (needs decisions):
- Where to run the heavy work (vuln scans, SBOM processing)? CI runner vs central scan cluster. Tradeoff: runner cost vs central queue latency.
- Authority model for break-glass: who can bypass image-signature failure and how do we track it?
- Runtime policy enforcement vs preflight-only: do we allow preflight-only for non-critical customers? (risk profile differs by tier)
- Handling air-gapped environments where registry/cosign can't hit public keys: need an offline signing/verification path + offline SBOM transfer.

Next steps / action items (owners):
- Prototype preflight container image with cosign+trivy+syft + small python orchestrator (Priya, ETA 3 weeks).
- Add tf-asserts.py proof-of-concept to infra-tools repo (Ethan, ETA 2 weeks).
- Draft Rego policy bundle for helm chart constraints (Maya, ETA 2 weeks).
- Plan SRE oncall playbook for rollback criteria + alert runbooks (SRE Oncall, ETA 1 week).
- Spike: offline signing flow for air-gapped installs (Luca, ETA 4 weeks).

Notes to self / quick wins:
- Start by enforcing image digest pinning and helm lint — cheap wins with low false-positive rate.
- Reuse existing artifact storage patterns used by hosted-api pipelines to avoid building new infra.
- Keep attestation format minimal JSON with fields we need for verification (digest, signer, sbom url, tf-plan-hash, timestamp, ci-job-id).

Appendix: quick commands
- verify image signature: cosign verify --key ci.pub registry.example.com/repo/image@sha256:...
- generate sbom: syft registry.example.com/repo/image:tag -o spdx-json > sbom.json
- trivy sbom scan: trivy sbom --input sbom.json --exit-code 1
- tf plan json: terraform plan -out tfplan && terraform show -json tfplan > plan.json

If you skim this doc and think of missing checks or a simpler approach for initial rollout, add a comment or drop a short PR in infra-tools. This is intentionally pragmatic: ship a small set of high-signal validations first, iterate after operational experience.

-- end draft --

title: first-90-days-playbook-alpha-cohort-notes-oct31-2024
preview:
Purpose: quick, pragmatic "first-90" notes from the alpha cohort onboarding experiments. This is a working doc — messy, contains action items, standup snippets, and interview/onboarding observations for iteration.

Summary of cohort: 5 new engineers started between Oct 7–21. Mix of SRE-ish and infra/CI skills. Goal was to validate a 90-day path emphasizing: (a) ramping to owner of a small infra service, (b) independent runbook edits and small production fixes, (c) comfort with deployment and alerting workflows.

What we ran (high level):
- 2-week bootcamp: infra fundamentals (networking in VPC, k8s minimal ops, runtime topology for serving nodes).
- Pair rotations across platform stack: autoscaler, monitoring, deployment pipelines.
- Weekly "health sync" with mentor + cohort retrospective.
- Shadow rotation on oncall for 4 shifts in first 30 days (observability-only with mentor present).

Observed wins (anecdotal):
- Faster ownership: two engineers made their first safe production change (config small change) on day 18. Good mentoring and pre-approved rollback policy helped.
- Docs improvement: newcomers flagged 7 small but critical doc gaps; 5 have PRs open (see linked git PR).
- Cross-team pairing: pairing with serving-runtime reduced friction on environment setup (time-to-first-boot down from ~6 hrs to ~2 hrs for some folks).

Pain points / surprises:
- Too many short-lived tickets for infra setup. New hires got drown in ephemeral tasks vs meaningful ownership. Action: consolidate setup tasks into a single checklist ticket (JIRA-PLAT-3578).
- Oncall exposure felt high for folks without prior SRE experience — even shadow shifts were stressful. Suggest softening first 90 to observability-only first 45.
- Confusing credential flow for private deployments: multiple steps, secret rotation required manual hub ops. Task: create a simplified helper script and sign-off process (owner: Lin Zhao, due 2024-11-18).

Interview & hiring notes (recent debriefs):
- Candidate A (back-end infra): strong systems background, lacked k8s debugging depth but high ramp potential. Recommendation: hire on IC2 with mentorship plan focusing k8s in first 6 weeks.
- Candidate B (SRE-focused): great incident postmortem thinking, weaker on infra-as-code. Recommendation: keep for mid-funnel (revisit if their infra IaC project shows maturity).
- Candidate C (platform tools): excellent product sensibility; would speed up docs & UX for onboarding flows. Strong hire.
- Notes to hiring panel: add a short troubleshooting exercise that mirrors our credential/agent setup (30 min) — many candidates excel on hypotheticals but stumble on our credential maze.

Standup excerpts (for traceability, raw):
- Oct 15: "blocked on vault policy - need perms; mentor pinged security; trying workaround with temporary token"
- Oct 21: "completed local cluster script; still failing deploy integration test on CI-3; suspect ENV mismatch"
- Oct 29: "did first oncall shadow — lots of alert noise; cleaned 2 alert rules, created JIRA-PLAT-3610 to tune thresholds"

Health pulse (cohort quick survey, N=5):

title: Obelisk Energy on‑prem hybrid cutover & Terraform playbook
preview:
Purpose:
This is a working playbook for Obelisk Energy’s private deployment (hybrid VPC + on‑prem site) cutover. Focus: sizing, architecture Q&A for network‑constrained sites, Terraform module call patterns, and an upgrade strategy that preserves model cache/KV across rolling kernel and runtime upgrades. This is intentionally rough — contains TODOs and open questions for SE/infra review.

Background/context: 
- Customer: Obelisk Energy (utility monitoring + outage prediction). Low tolerance for downtime; primary inference traffic is near‑real‑time grid telemetry and edge alerting.
- Desired topology: Two on‑prem inference clusters (primary in Site A, DR in Site B) connected to Redwood private control plane over a managed VPC peering link. Some racks are air‑gapped during maintenance windows and will have intermittent egress to control plane.
- Constraints: MTU must be aligned with existing SCADA network (1500), limited public IPs, strict egress filtering, HSM/KMS on prem for key material.

High‑level goals: 
1) Zero data exfiltration outside allowed endpoints (control plane + KMS).
2) <10 ms p99 local latency for single‑region inference for short sequences (<512 tokens).
3) Predictable commit cost: capacity + burst plan for seasonal peaks.
4) Seamless upgrades with <5 minute degraded window per node and automated fallback to smaller model variants.

Sizing assumptions (initial proposal): 
- Workload: mixture of chat-style and streaming telemetry summarization. Average request size ~120 tokens; p50 token generation per request ~30 tokens.
- Peak concurrent inferences (Site A): 320 streams; Site B (DR, warm): 80 streams.
- Model: resizable family based on opensource transformer 7B baseline; fallback to 2.7B quantized variant for severe capacity constraints.
- Target latency SLO: single query end-to-end 100 ms p95, 250 ms p99 (local).

Hardware proposal (per site): 
- 8x nodes per production rack; each node: 1x dual‑socket CPU, 512GB RAM, 2x 1.6TB NVMe (RAID1 for OS + runtime), 1x NVIDIA A10G per node (initial); option to upgrade to H100 later.
- KV cache placement: local NVMe cache + optional shared NFS for large prefix cache (if using stateful replay workloads). Prefer local NVMe for latency.
- Disk sizing: runtime + models require ~450GB per 7B model (float16). Leave 1TB free for multiple variants and temporary artifacts.

Terraform module notes (recommended module call pattern): 
- Root module: modules/obelisk/onprem-cluster-main.tf which composes: modules/ri_network, modules/ri_runtime_node, modules/ri_kms_connector, modules/ri_monitoring.
- Key variables to expose: cluster_name, site_cidr_list (list), mgmt_subnet, node_count_per_rack, gpu_type, local_nvme_class, kms_mode (vault|cloud_kms|hsm), egress_allowed_ips (list), control_plane_endpoints (list).
- Outputs to wire to control plane: cluster_id, node_pool_ids, kube_api_endpoints, storage_class_name, kms_connector_id.
- Terraform state: use remote state backend (s3-compatible object store within Obelisk private infra) with state locking via Consul. Example backend config is in modules/obelisk/backend.tf (see linked PR).
- Important: keep separate state files for network and compute to allow independent rollouts.

Terraform variables snippet (doc-only): 
- variable \"node_count_per_rack\" { type = number default = 8 }
- variable \"gpu_type\" { type = string default = \"nvidia-a10g\" }
- variable \"kms_mode\" { type = string default = \"hsm\" }

Module implementation notes / pitfalls discovered in PoC: 
- When using on‑prem HSM, Terraform provider for HSM auth requires a short‑lived token that must be refreshed by the operator. We added a local exec helper that retrieves a token via a signed challenge. See GH PR/187 for the helper script.
- network security groups must include explicit control_plane_endpoints; any DNS fallback to public resolvers was blocked by the customer FW and caused runtime registration failures in the PoC.
- Kernel versions: runtime v2.4.1 used GPU kernels requiring Linux kernel >=5.15. Some customer nodes booted to 5.10 and triggered CUDA compatibility errors. Add kernel check in preflight.

Preflight checklist (ops): 
- [ ] Verify OS kernel >= 5.15 on all nodes.
- [ ] Confirm MTU = 1500 across OOB and data networks.
- [ ] Run rust-based runtime probe to verify GPU visibility and driver >= 525.85.
- [ ] Validate outbound TLS to control plane endpoints on TCP 443 (and port 24443 for metrics ingestion) from a node in each rack.
- [ ] Validate HSM connectivity using the HSM smoke tool.
- [ ] Ensure Terraform remote state lock test completes.

Cutover strategy (phased, minimal blast radius): 
1) Dry run in isolated VLAN with a single node pool and synthetic traffic for 48 hours.
2) Provision initial production pool (25% of target nodes) and register with control plane in read‑only mode. Exercise canary model (2.7B quantized) behind feature flag.
3) Gradually scale node pool to 50%, then 100% while observing token-level cost metrics and KV hit rate.
4) For runtime upgrades: rolling upgrade by node with local cache warmup: drain node, update runtime package, start node, wait for KV cache repopulation (tracked via cache-misses metric) before returning to service.
5) If runtime upgrade causes widespread errors, automatic routing policy will fall back traffic to smaller quantized model hosted in dedicated pool for up to 2 hours while we investigate.

Upgrade-specific notes: 
- Preserve model artifacts across node reboots by keeping /var/lib/redwood/models on local NVMe with systemd unit to untar preloaded bundles during boot. Avoid pulling large bundles during cutover windows.
- When moving from A10G to H100, expect a change in kernel module and CUDA driver: coordinate with SRE to perform driver upgrade in a narrow maintenance window. Plan for a capacity buffer of ~20% during driver rollouts because H100 throughput per GPU may differ for different kernels.

Instrumenting & SLOs: 
- Metrics to wire to Redwood console: local_p99_latency_ms, model_cache_hit_rate, batch_size, gpu_utilization, kv_miss_rate, runtime_errors_total.
- Alerts: kv_miss_rate > 5% for >10m; model_cache_hit_rate drop >15% within 15m; node_gpu_temperature > 85C; driver_crash_loop > 3 restarts in 5m.

Open questions / TODOs: 
- Customer request: can we support fully air-gapped initial sync of model artifacts over physical media? (TODO: confirm import signing workflow).
- Need final confirmation on HSM vendor and whether PKCS#11 is exposed to Terraform provider. (Action: Marco to reach out to Obelisk infra team).
- Decide whether to expose per-route canary percentage via Terraform or keep rollout in control plane only. Pros/cons in body.

Rollback plan (brief): 
- If cutover fails at scale, we will: 1) revert traffic routing to control plane-hosted fallback pool; 2) tear down new node pool (Terraform destroy) if misconfig is discovered; 3) if stateful mismatch, restore model artifacts from pre-cutover snapshot located on on‑prem object store.

Appendix: PoC notes and commands used
- Sample Terraform apply for network-only: terraform apply -var-file=env/obelisk-network.tfvars -target=module.ri_network
- Runtime health check: /usr/local/bin/redwood-runtime-check --endpoint https://controlplane.example.net --node-id ${NODE_ID}
- HSM token retrieval (ops): ./scripts/get_hsm_token.sh --challenge /tmp/chal.bin | jq -r .token

References and artifacts: 
- Confluence design doc (linked).
- GH PR 187: terraform backend and hsm helper.
- Jira tickets: JIRA-4521 (onprem reqs), JIRA-4783 (cutover automation).

Notes to self (informal): 
- Keep playbook modular so customer infra team can slot in their existing Consul/Vault patterns.
- Avoid NFS for KV unless absolutely necessary — latency killed our early tests.
- If customer insists on A100/H100 mix, ensure runtime can select kernel paths per node pool.

End of draft.

Status updates: 
- 2024-10-02: added Terraform backend recommendations and HSM helper link.
- 2024-10-12: adjusted disk sizing after storage PoC (increased model reserve to 1TB).
- 2024-10-18: updated kernel preflight check and listed linked GH PR.

title: Zephyr Biotech infra acceptance criteria and upgrade path
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This working brief captures deployment acceptance criteria, sizing assumptions, Terraform module considerations, and a staged upgrade/rollback path for Zephyr Biotech’s private inference deployment (VPC + optional on-prem GPU cluster). Intended audience: Zephyr infra leads, Redwood deployment engineers, solutions eng, and SREs. This is a draft meant to consolidate technical Q&A and operational constraints before the formal playbook is produced.

High-level goals/constraints from Zephyr:
- Latency SLO: 95th percentile response latency < 120ms for single-request token-forward passes for clinical triage model (context length 1024).
- Throughput target: baseline 600 requests/min sustained across model endpoints; peak bursts up to 1,800 requests/min for 15-minute windows.
- Data controls: all inference traffic and logs must remain within customer VPC; key material stored in customer KMS (AWS CloudHSM or Azure KeyVault).
- Air-gap requirement for certain environments: offline model artifact ingestion via signed transport bundle.
- Minimum HA: regional failover within 3 availability zones; optional single-site on-prem N+1.

Assumptions encoded in sizing model:
- Typical request = 64 tokens generated on average; seq length distribution: 80% <= 512, 15% 513-1024, 5% >1024.
- Baseline model: 13B dense, CPU/GPU profile measured on Redwood runtime: ~45 tokens/s per A100-40GB for typical mix with autotuned kernels.
- Inference pipeline includes pre-tokenize, tokenizer, model kernel, postprocess. 20% of requests require optional reranking step (lightweight).
- KV cache hit rate expected 12% across requests (prefix reuse for multi-turn flows).

Sizing recommendation (initial):
- Cloud VPC (recommended fast path): 6 x r5.8xlarge-like frontend nodes (for batching, tokenization, API layer).
- GPU fleet: 6 x A100-40GB nodes (dedicated model shards or replicated single-shard serving depending on throughput curves). This gives headroom for 2x baseline throughput with concurrency and batching.
- Storage: 2 x high-IO PS volumes (NFS or EFS) for model artifacts + 3 x S3-compatible object store for remote state and signed bundles.
- Networking: 25 Gbps intra-node NICs recommended; jumbo frames enabled for lower CPU usage on KV transfers.
- On-prem variant: 4 x GPU nodes (A100-equivalent) in initial phase with plan to scale to 8 nodes on sustained demand. Rack-level redundancy and dual ToR recommended.

Performance margins and contingency:
- Target capacity to be provisioned at ~40-50% of theoretical max to allow for sudden bursts, model warmup, and kernel variability.
- Configure autoscaling (if cloud) with step policy: +2 GPUs per 5-minute sustained >75% utilization for 10 minutes.

TF module notes and patterns:
- Module layout: separate modules for network (vpc, subnets, routes), compute (gpu node groups), storage (artifact store, remote state), k8s cluster (optional), and kms integration.
- State: use remote state in customer-managed S3 with DynamoDB locking (or equivalent blob-locking). Do not store keys/credentials in TF state outputs.
- Versioning: modules must follow semver; pinned provider versions in root module; CI gating on module bump with acceptance test run.
- Idempotency: modules must be idempotent for partial apply failure scenarios; use create_before_destroy on resources where possible (e.g., new node pools) to avoid downtime.
- Secrets: leverage external secret manager provider rather than passing secrets through TF variables. Integrate with customer KMS -> Redwood deployment reads sealed secrets at runtime.
- Bootstrapping for air-gapped: TF should accept artifact checksums and object-store URIs; for fully air-gapped, provide a local artifact bundle import module that populates the artifact store from a signed archive.

Compatibility matrix (important for Zephyr):
- Kernel: RHEL 8.6+ or Ubuntu 22.04 recommended. Avoid Linux kernels older than 5.10 for PCIe/NIC stability with newer GPUs.
- CUDA/ROCm: if using NVIDIA, CUDA 11.8 verified with our runtime; ROCm 5.x support is experimental — avoid for initial rollout.
- Drivers: NVIDIA drivers >= 525.x recommended for A100 series. Documented driver matches will be included in final playbook.

Acceptance criteria (must pass before handover):
1) Functional: API endpoints respond with correct structured JSON for a canonical prompt set; unit tests cover tokenizer and reranker flows.
2) Performance: synthetic load test mimicking 600 req/min sustained with 95th < 120ms and < 80% GPU utilization on baseline cluster.
3) Failover: simulate AZ loss and confirm routing/fallback within documented window, and no data leakage across AZs.
4) Security: KMS-integrated keys for model decrypt/encrypt; audit logs for key usage present for 30 days. No plaintext model artifacts left on ephemeral nodes after job termination.
5) Upgrade safety: blue/green or canary path validated on staging cluster with roll-forward time < 20 minutes and automatic rollback on error thresholds.

Upgrade and rollback path (staged):
- Pre-upgrade checks: collect cluster health metrics, verify backups of remote state, verify signatures and checksums of model artifacts, snapshot critical volumes.
- Canary phase: deploy new runtime (or new TF module version) to 10% of traffic using routing policy; run smoke tests for 30 minutes; verify latency, error rate, token utilization, and quality metrics.
- Scale-out phase: if canary passes, rollout to 50% for 60 minutes, then to 100% in 2 phases with pause windows for validation.
- Rollback criteria: >1% increase in 5xxs, >10% increase in 95th latency vs baseline, or any quality regression detected by automated evals on representative prompt set. Rollback must be automated by reapplying previous module version and draining new nodes; plan for quick route switch to previous model variant.
- Post-upgrade validation: run end-to-end evals, confirm observability dashboards reflect expected token counts and cost deltas, and notify Zephyr via configured webhook.

Runbook snippets (operational play):
- Node replacement: mark node cordon, drain, verify KV caches flushed to persistent store if required, replace node via TF module with new instance template id.

- Preference for model residency: keep model artifacts in customer-managed S3 or in local encrypted filesystem on prem? Cost/latency tradeoffs need confirmation.

- Risk: Air-gapped artifact ingestion creates drift if not reconciled with versioned manifests. Mitigation: signed manifest with monotonic version and automated artifact checksum verification in TF preflight.

1) Validate kernel/driver matrix with Zephyr’s infra team (calendar invite: ops deep-dive).
2) Run a synthetic load test runbook in staging that uses Zephyr prompt set (JIRA-DEP-923).

Purpose and scope:
This working brief captures deployment acceptance criteria, sizing assumptions, Terraform module considerations, and a staged upgrade/rollback path for Zephyr Biotech’s private inference deployment (VPC + optional on-prem GPU cluster). Intended audience: Zephyr infra leads, Redwood deployment engineers, solutions eng, and SREs. This is a draft meant to consolidate technical Q&A and operational constraints before the formal playbook is produced.

High-level goals/constraints from Zephyr:
- Latency SLO: 95th percentile response latency < 120ms for single-request token-forward passes for clinical triage model (context length 1024).
- Throughput target: baseline 600 requests/min sustained across model endpoints; peak bursts up to 1,800 requests/min for 15-minute windows.
- Data controls: all inference traffic and logs must remain within customer VPC; key material stored in customer KMS (AWS CloudHSM or Azure KeyVault).
- Air-gap requirement for certain environments: offline model artifact ingestion via signed transport bundle.
- Minimum HA: regional failover within 3 availability zones; optional single-site on-prem N+1.

Assumptions encoded in sizing model:
- Typical request = 64 tokens generated on average; seq length distribution: 80% <= 512, 15% 513-1024, 5% >1024.
- Baseline model: 13B dense, CPU/GPU profile measured on Redwood runtime: ~45 tokens/s per A100-40GB for typical mix with autotuned kernels.
- Inference pipeline includes pre-tokenize, tokenizer, model kernel, postprocess. 20% of requests require optional reranking step (lightweight).
- KV cache hit rate expected 12% across requests (prefix reuse for multi-turn flows).

Sizing recommendation (initial):
- Cloud VPC (recommended fast path): 6 x r5.8xlarge-like frontend nodes (for batching, tokenization, API layer).
- GPU fleet: 6 x A100-40GB nodes (dedicated model shards or replicated single-shard serving depending on throughput curves). This gives headroom for 2x baseline throughput with concurrency and batching.
- Storage: 2 x high-IO PS volumes (NFS or EFS) for model artifacts + 3 x S3-compatible object store for remote state and signed bundles.
- Networking: 25 Gbps intra-node NICs recommended; jumbo frames enabled for lower CPU usage on KV transfers.
- On-prem variant: 4 x GPU nodes (A100-equivalent) in initial phase with plan to scale to 8 nodes on sustained demand. Rack-level redundancy and dual ToR recommended.

Performance margins and contingency:
- Target capacity to be provisioned at ~40-50% of theoretical max to allow for sudden bursts, model warmup, and kernel variability.
- Configure autoscaling (if cloud) with step policy: +2 GPUs per 5-minute sustained >75% utilization for 10 minutes.

TF module notes and patterns:
- Module layout: separate modules for network (vpc, subnets, routes), compute (gpu node groups), storage (artifact store, remote state), k8s cluster (optional), and kms integration.
- State: use remote state in customer-managed S3 with DynamoDB locking (or equivalent blob-locking). Do not store keys/credentials in TF state outputs.
- Versioning: modules must follow semver; pinned provider versions in root module; CI gating on module bump with acceptance test run.
- Idempotency: modules must be idempotent for partial apply failure scenarios; use create_before_destroy on resources where possible (e.g., new node pools) to avoid downtime.
- Secrets: leverage external secret manager provider rather than passing secrets through TF variables. Integrate with customer KMS -> Redwood deployment reads sealed secrets at runtime.
- Bootstrapping for air-gapped: TF should accept artifact checksums and object-store URIs; for fully air-gapped, provide a local artifact bundle import module that populates the artifact store from a signed archive.

Compatibility matrix (important for Zephyr):
- Kernel: RHEL 8.6+ or Ubuntu 22.04 recommended. Avoid Linux kernels older than 5.10 for PCIe/NIC stability with newer GPUs.
- CUDA/ROCm: if using NVIDIA, CUDA 11.8 verified with our runtime; ROCm 5.x support is experimental — avoid for initial rollout.
- Drivers: NVIDIA drivers >= 525.x recommended for A100 series. Documented driver matches will be included in final playbook.

1) Functional: API endpoints respond with correct structured JSON for a canonical prompt set; unit tests cover tokenizer and reranker flows.
2) Performance: synthetic load test mimicking 600 req/min sustained with 95th < 120ms and < 80% GPU utilization on baseline cluster.
3) Failover: simulate AZ loss and confirm routing/fallback within documented window, and no data leakage across AZs.

title: Monarch Railways on‑prem cutover & capacity playbook (WIP)
preview:
Purpose
This is a working playbook for Monarch Railways' private deployment (on‑prem VPC + air‑gapped optionality). Focus: initial capacity sizing, minimal‑maintenance Terraform modules, cutover sequence that minimizes train‑control downtime, and clear rollback windows. This is a WIP — technical assumptions and unknowns are called out in the Questions section.

Executive summary (1–2 lines)
Monarch requires an on‑prem inference cluster to run rule‑based and LLM routing for timetable anomaly detection with a 50 ms P95 inference SLA for safety alerts. Proposal: 8 dedicated A100‑style GPUs (or equivalent) behind a local VPC, with two availability zones (rack pairs) and a short canary cutover plan with traffic shaping.

Key customer constraints
- No external model weights allowed offsite (weights must live behind customer firewall).
- Maintenance window limited to 02:00–05:00 local (4 total hours) on weekends for cutover.
- Primary latency target: P95 <= 50 ms for 256 token requests; burst tolerance up to 4x baseline for 5 minutes.
- KMS integration via on‑prem HSM — Redwood must support customer KMS endpoints.

Assumptions (explicit)
- Baseline QPS: 60 requests/sec steady; peak bursts up to 240 rps for incident windows.
- Average tokens per request: 120 input + 80 generated (200 total).
- Model family: 7B–13B open weights (quantized to int8 or nf4).
- GPU performance estimate (per device): 60 tokens/sec sustained for this model profile under our runtime with quantization.
- KV cache hit rate for steady state: 6% (conservative).

Sizing rationale (calculation summary)
- Steady throughput tokens/sec: 60 rps * 200 tokens = 12,000 tokens/sec.
- Per‑GPU sustained tokens/sec: ~60 -> GPUs required steady = 12,000 / 60 = 200 => clearly unrealistic unless batching reduces cost.
- Apply batching & caching: expected effective improvement x20 in tokens/sec per GPU during typical traffic shape (continuous batching, prefix cache). Revised per‑GPU effective tokens/sec: 1,200.
- Revised GPUs = 12,000 / 1,200 = 10 GPUs. Add 20% headroom => 12 GPUs.
- For burst tolerance (4x for 5 minutes) use autoscale/queueing: reserve 8 GPUs always, allow burst pool of 6 GPUs to start from cold within 60s using warm‑standby images. Final proposal: 14 GPUs logical; suggested buy: 16 nodes to support maintenance/evacuation.

Proposed topology (high level)
- Two rack zones (AZ1, AZ2) on customer colocation network.
- Control plane (managed by Redwood private control plane inside customer VPC): 3 control VMs (etcd/leader) spread across racks.
- Serving pool: 16 GPU nodes (8 per rack target distribution), each node runs our runtime in containerd with pinned NIC interrupts and NUMA affinity.
- Redis cluster (caching + KV): 3 nodes on customer VMs behind VPC.
- Ingress LB: local HA proxy pair in front of runtime, with health checks and circuit breaker policies.
- KMS gateway: connection to customer HSM via TLS mutual auth; no secrets stored outside HSM.

Terraform modules notes (operational guidance)
- Goal: keep modules minimal and idempotent. Provide two modules to the customer: "redwood-private-controlplane" and "redwood-serving-pool". Keep heavy‑lifting in internal provider.
- Recommended variables:
  - cluster_name (string)
  - rack_labels (list of strings)
  - gpu_node_count (number) default = 16
  - maintenance_windows (map)
  - kms_endpoint (string)
  - networking: mgmt_subnet, serving_subnet, db_subnet (strings)
- Outputs to expose to customer: control_plane_ips (list), serving_lb_ips (list), redis_ips (list), tfstate_bucket (if using remote state)
- State strategy: remote state backend inside customer's S3‑compatible object store (minio) or locking via Dynamo style API that their colo supports — avoid public backends.
- Module constraints: avoid changing node_serialization ordering (we need predictable node names for maintenance).
- Versioning: tag module versions and change logs; upgrade path must preserve IPs for control plane to avoid rekeying.

Terraform sample snippet (note: explanatory only)
variable "gpu_node_count" { default = 16 }
module "redwood_serving_pool" {
  source = "git::ssh://git@github.com/redwood-inference/terraform-modules//serving_pool?ref=v0.5.1"
  cluster_name = var.cluster_name
  rack_labels  = var.rack_labels
  node_count   = var.gpu_node_count
  image_id     = var.gpu_image_id
  kms_endpoint = var.kms_endpoint
}

Upgrade / cutover plan (stepwise)
- Precutover (T‑7d to T‑1d):
  - Finalize TF variables and run `terraform plan` in dry run with customer infra team.
  - Deploy control plane (3 nodes) in AZ1/AZ2 (staggered).
  - Validate HSM/KMS handshake with test keys and an isolated test app.
  - Deploy 2 serving nodes as canaries and register in LB with 0% traffic. Run perf tests for 48 hours.
  - Run synthetic traffic to measure real P95 and batch efficiency; iterate on model quantization param in image.

- Cutover weekend (maintenance window: 02:00–05:00)
  1) 02:00–02:20: Snapshot existing production configuration and notify all upstream systems.
  2) 02:20–02:45: Provision full serving pool (target count nodes) via Terraform apply with parallelism=5; bring nodes to 'standby' mode.
  3) 02:45–03:15: Run smoke tests on all nodes (model load, KV access, token throughput).
  4) 03:15–03:35: Canary release: route 5% of traffic to new on‑prem pool using traffic shaping at L7. Monitor P95 and error rates.
  5) 03:35–04:15: Gradual ramp to 50% then 100% (if metrics stable). Keep rollback window open until 05:00.
  6) 04:15–04:45: If any regression >5% in P95 or error spikes >1% above baseline, rollback to old endpoint (see rollback below).
  7) 04:45–05:00: Postcutover validation and finalize DNS / traffic records.

Rollback checklist (fast rollback)
- Repoint LB to previous endpoints (we keep old endpoints warm for 1 hour after cutover).
- Run `terraform destroy` only for recently created nodes (if needed) using resource targeting to avoid touching control plane.
- Re‑enable upstream routing and notify incident channel

Open questions / risks (action owners assigned inline)
- HSM connection: customer HSM supports only IP allowlist; we need static egress IP for control plane. (Owner: Marcus)
- Cold start of GPUs under 4x burst — can we warm images quickly enough? Need to validate warm‑standby time < 60s. (Owner: Sofia)
- Network QoS: customer needs L2 mapping for interrupt affinity to keep NIC latency < 300 µs. (Owner: Ethan)
- Regulatory edge: some models might still be restricted by customer compliance; catalog approved model list must be locked. (Owner: Riley)

Monitoring & observability (minimal initial deployment)
- Ship Fluentd logs to customer's log cluster (no external forwarding).
- Prometheus + node exporter + custom runtime metrics. Sample alert rules: P95 > 50ms (1m window), error_rate > 1% (5m), GPU_mem_pressure > 90% (2m).
- Dashboard: per‑route token cost, per‑node latency histogram, KV cache hit rate.

Operational runbook snippets
- Replace GPU node:
  1) Cordon & drain node in orchestration layer (drain waits for in‑flight tokens to finish).
  2) Remove from LB and wait for zero inflight requests.
  3) Run terraform apply with node replacement image variable changed.
  4) Validate smoke tests, then mark node back in service.

- Emergency key rotation with HSM:
  - Use the on‑prem KMS gateway to request a new data key. Run rotation scripts in `scripts/rotate-keys.sh` (provided) that call HSM API and rotate local keystore.

Appendix: quick perf checklist for final sizing signoff
- Run 30m sustained traffic at baseline QPS; measure tokens/sec per GPU and effective batch factor.
- Run 10m burst test @4x QPS and measure warm‑start time for new nodes.
- Test HSM handshake and key rotation without actual model decryption using stubbed test keys.

Notes / short TODOs (owner)
- [Marcus] Confirm static egress IP for control plane to share with customer networking.
- [Sofia] Run warm‑start experiment with int8 build on prototype rack by 2024‑09‑30.
- [Ethan] Draft QoS pinning instructions for customer's colo engineers.
- [Riley] Compile compliance list of approved model artifacts and upload to Confluence.

Contact points
- Redwood lead: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.ai)
- Customer infra: Anika Das (Monarch Railways) — onboarding PM

End of WIP playbook. Intend to iterate after perf runs and first TF dry‑run with customer infra team.

title: Egressless proxy patterns for inference VPC onboarding (draft)
preview:
Purpose

This draft documents a set of patterns and an operational checklist for onboarding customers that require "egressless" or "NATless" VPCs for inference workloads (on-prem or VPC with strict egress controls). The aim is to give a pragmatic path: secure customer activation, minimal runbook surface for SREs, Terraform module notes for infra teams, and an upgrade/rollback sequencing plan that avoids downtime for model-serving endpoints.

Scope & assumptions

- Scope: LLM inference endpoints (Redwood runtime) deployed in customer-managed networks where outbound egress to public Internet is forbidden or heavily inspected. Covers both fully air-gapped on-prem installs and cloud VPCs with egress restrictions.
- Assumes customers permit at least an authenticated, single-direction channel for package + telemetry transfers via an approved gateway (APIGW, signed bundle, or removable media). If not possible, adaptations noted below.
- Not prescriptive on orchestration (K8s vs VM), but includes idioms for both.

High-level pattern choices (tradeoffs)

1) Signed model bundles + local runtime (no runtime egress)
   - What: Push model artifacts (quantized weights, tokenizer) as signed, versioned bundles into the customer environment. Runtime performs all inference locally; telemetry is batched and exported only if permitted.
   - Pros: Strong data residency; deterministic runtime behavior; minimal network surface.
   - Cons: Deployment cadence requires bundle distribution process; model updates require coordinated rollouts.

2) Sidecar inference-proxy with approved egress gateway (egress-constrained)
   - What: Deploy a small sidecar that mediates model access and only allows traffic to a customer-approved gateway (reverse proxy or TLS-terminated channel). Gateway enforces allowlists, logging, and DPI.
   - Pros: Easier updates (models remain in hosted repo behind gateway); central observability.
   - Cons: Requires customer gateway configuration; still constrained by legal egress policies.

3) Local inference + ephemeral validation bridge (one-time outbound)
   - What: Use a temporary, tightly-scoped outbound channel during onboarding for health checks, model verification signatures, and initial metrics upload. After validation, the channel is closed and operation continues offline.
   - Pros: Simplifies onboarding validation; reduces long-lived egress.
   - Cons: Requires precise scheduling + documented proof-of-closure.

Pattern selection guidance

- Greenfield, regulated customers (no persistent egress allowed): Choose (1) Signed bundles. Provide a per-tenant signing key and verification chain.
- Customers allowing a hardcoded gateway (enterprise proxy): Choose (2). Recommend mTLS and token binding to instance identity.
- Customers that want rapid POC then lock down: Start with (3) then transition to (1).

Minimal Terraform module notes (opinionated)

- Module separation: network/ (customer hooks), runtime/ (k8s/instance templates), distro/ (bundle distribution). Keep modules small and composable so customers can override network primitives.
- Inputs to highlight in module README: allowed_gateway_ips, bundle_signing_key_id, telemetry_batch_window_seconds, local_cache_size_gb, storage_class_name, init_validation_window_seconds.
- Outputs to expose: bootstrap_token (one-time), model_bundle_version, validation_report_s3_uri (or path), local_checksum_status.
- Example lifecycle hook: a terraform null_resource that calls a signed-bundle verification script post-deploy; fail fast if signature mismatch.

Onboarding checklist (stepwise)

1) Pre-kickoff (account manager + solutions engineer)
   - Collect network constraints: egress allowed? allowed gateway FQDN/IPs? port filters? IDS/IPS expectations?
   - Determine acceptance criteria for validation: sample prompts, latency targets, perf profile (p50/p95), telemetry retention.

2) Planning call (customer infra + Redwood SRE)
   - Confirm pattern choice (1/2/3).
   - Exchange signing public key (if signed bundles). Establish KMS/HSM plan for key custody.
   - Agree rollout windows, rollback triggers, and communication channels.

3) Terraform scaffolding & permissions
   - Provide customer a lean Terraform example that instantiates runtime nodes, local storage (PVCs), and the verification null_resource.
   - Customer validates that bootstrap role can create the required compute artifacts.

4) Initial deploy (canary)
   - Deploy a single canary tenant with constrained traffic.
   - Run init validation: checksum/signature verify, model dry-run (small batch), and telemetry stamping.
   - Collect validation report and customer signoff.

5) Bulk rollout
   - Gradual scale: increase tenant instances by 2x per window with health probes. Monitor memory/KV cache pressure closely.

6) Lockdown (if temporary bridge used)
   - Execute egress bridge closure. Obtain signed confirmation from customer's network team.

7) Handover
   - Deliver runbook: backup/restore of local bundles, key rotation steps, emergency debug checklist.

Upgrade and rollback sequencing (zero-downtime principles)

- Non-disruptive bundle upgrade flow (recommended for signed bundles):
  1. Publish new model bundle to distribution channel with version tag.
  2. Canary: add new bundle to 1-2 canary nodes (node-level toggles in runtime). Run smoke tests.
  3. Gradual traffic shift: redirect a % of traffic to nodes with new bundle. Monitor quality/regression metrics.
  4. Full flip after 95% of checks pass for a defined observation window.
  5. Decommission old bundle when safe.

- Rollback: Immediately re-route traffic back to previous version and mark new bundle as quarantined. Keep quarantine TTL (72h) while root cause analysis runs. Provide automated job to re-sign and re-publish if root cause is artifact corruption.

Edge cases & gotchas

- Large model sizes: For constrained networks, pre-stage a small warm-start quantized variant to reduce initial transfer times.
- KV cache warming: Cold starts can spike latency; include a synthetic warmup run during canary validation.
- Key compromise: Rotate signing key and publish revocation list. Have fallback acceptance of previous key for a short overlap window.
- Storage quotas: Ensure PVC sizing in Terraform includes room for two full bundles (current + next) to allow atomic swap.

Operational runbook snippets (quick)

- Verify bundle signature: sh ./tools/verify_bundle.sh --bundle /mnt/bundles/model-v2.tar --key-id xxxxx
- Canary smoke: curl -sS --unix-socket /var/run/redwood.sock "/v1/generate" -d '{"prompt":"warmup","max_tokens":8}'
- Emergency rollback: run terraform apply -var 'target_bundle=model-v1' modules/runtime/rollback.tf

Open questions / follow-ups

- Explore automation for signed-bundle distribution over removable media (secure checksum manifest + QR verification) for fully air-gapped customers.
- Add Terraform test harness (kitchen-terraform style) to validate null_resource hooks in CI before shipping examples.
- Confirm SRE on-call playbook for incidents where local runtime degrades without network telemetry.

Appendix: useful references

- internal link: confluence:private-deployments/signed-bundle-spec-v1
- internal PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/cloud-terraform/pull/642
- related Jira: JIRA-PLAT-4387 (egressless onboarding epic)

Notes

This is intentionally prescriptive but lightweight — aim is to give sales/SREs an executable path without heavy engineering changes. Feedback welcome: if we adopt one of these patterns broadly we should convert the minimal terraform snippets into reusable modules in the public repo and create a test-suite that runs in customer-like emulation labs.

Scope & assumptions

- Scope: LLM inference endpoints (Redwood runtime) deployed in customer-managed networks where outbound egress to public Internet is forbidden or heavily inspected. Covers both fully air-gapped on-prem installs and cloud VPCs with egress restrictions.
