- Not prescriptive on orchestration (K8s vs VM), but includes idioms for both.

High-level pattern choices (tradeoffs)

1) Signed model bundles + local runtime (no runtime egress)
   - What: Push model artifacts (quantized weights, tokenizer) as signed, versioned bundles into the customer environment. Runtime performs all inference locally; telemetry is batched and exported only if permitted.
   - Pros: Strong data residency; deterministic runtime behavior; minimal network surface.
   - Cons: Deployment cadence requires bundle distribution process; model updates require coordinated rollouts.

2) Sidecar inference-proxy with approved egress gateway (egress-constrained)
   - What: Deploy a small sidecar that mediates model access and only allows traffic to a customer-approved gateway (reverse proxy or TLS-terminated channel). Gateway enforces allowlists, logging, and DPI.

title: Latency-aware variant scheduling and regional spillover
preview:
Purpose:
This note captures a pragmatic approach for latency-aware scheduling of model variants, tiered capacity handling, and a lightweight regional spillover handshake for the Redwood control plane. Goal is an operationally simple, observable flow that minimizes user-facing latency spikes while preserving cost predictability.

High-level goals:
- Keep 95th/99th percentile latency within SLOs for high-priority routes by using model variants dynamically.
- Avoid hard failures: prefer degraded-quality responses over errors when possible.
- Bound cross-region traffic: spillover only when local capacity is exhausted beyond configurable thresholds.
- Make decisions explainable and observable (routing reasons, metrics emitted).

Assumptions:
- We maintain a catalog of model variants with metadata: cost_score, latency_profile (p50/p95 for typical sequence lengths), compatibility tags (e.g., llm-v1, chat-format), and quality_delta vs baseline.
- Each region exposes real-time capacity tokens per tier: reserved, shared, burst. Tokens decrement as requests are accepted.
- Request-level metadata includes priority, max_acceptable_quality_loss (soft budget), latency_budget_ms, seq_length_estimate, and region_hint.

Key primitives:
1) Variant descriptor (example, stored in config):
- variant_id: rdx-gpt-large-8bit
- quality_tier: high | medium | low
- cost_score: numeric (~cost per token)
- latency_profile: {short: p50/p95, medium: p50/p95, long: p50/p95} (we store as strings in this doc but control plane stores structured)
- fallback_rank: integer (lower = try sooner)

2) Capacity tiers per region:
- reserved: customer-dedicated, guaranteed throughput
- shared: pooled capacity, subject to SLOs
- burst: opportunistic, best-effort, inexpensive to evict

3) Routing decisions emit reasons: e.g., "chosen_variant=rdx-gpt-medium | reason=latency_budget | region=us-west1 | capacity=tier:reserved"

Routing heuristic (core idea):
- Evaluate latency feasibility set: which variants can meet request.latency_budget_ms for the request.seq_length_estimate given region latency_profile and current queueing estimate? If none, consider spillover or degrade quality.
- Score each feasible variant by score = alpha * (1 / latency_estimate) + beta * (1 / cost_score) + gamma * quality_tier_weight + delta * affinity_bonus. Weights configurable by route.
- Apply capacity filter: prefer variants backed by reserved tokens in-ring; if reserved exhausted, consider shared; if shared exhausted and request.priority >= high, consider burst; else consider regional spillover.

Regional spillover policy (handshake):
- Spillover only when local shared+burst capacity < min_spill_threshold (configurable percent of expected baseline). We avoid spilling because cross-region adds tail latency and egress cost.
- Spill request to a nearby region with an explicit handshake: control plane issues a short-lived "spill token" to remote region, remote region validates token and consumes one of its burst tokens. This prevents silent uncontrolled cross-region consumption.
- The handshake is lightweight REST: POST /spill/offer {origin_region, route_id, request_id, ttl_ms, expected_cost_budget}. Remote returns ACCEPT/REJECT, and reason. Control plane logs both ends.
- Circuit breaker: if spill accept rate drops below 80% for >1min, origin region backs off and increases degrade rate (move to lower-quality variants locally).

Model variant degradation ladder (example):
- High quality (rdx-qa-large-fp16) -> Medium (rdx-gpt-medium-8bit) -> Low (rdx-small-4bit) -> Rerank-only / cache-hit -> Synthetic truncation + shorter context -> Graceful error (last resort)
- Each step has an associated expected quality_delta and cost saving. We track per-route tolerances (some routes cannot degrade beyond medium).

Control plane endpoints (sketch of responsibilities):
- POST /route/decide -> returns routing decision with chosen_variant_id, target_region, chosen_tier, tokens_reserved, decision_reason. Must be idempotent for retries.
- GET /region/capacity -> returns current tokens per tier, and a short health_status string.
- POST /spill/offer -> remote region accepts/rejects spill token. See handshake above.
- POST /variant/register -> admin route for new variant metadata and latency_profile updates.
- GET /explain?request_id= -> returns routing trace (used by console for per-request debug).

Observable signals (must emit):
- routing.decision (chosen_variant, region, tier, reason)
- routing.latency_estimate (ms)
- capacity.tokens.remaining (region, tier)
- spill.offer.{sent,accepted,rejected} counters and latencies
- degrade.step.{from_variant,to_variant,route}
- quality.regression_alert if downstream evals show quality loss beyond threshold when variants are used in fallback.

Playbook (operational flow for an elevated incident):
1) Identify: alert triggers when origin spill rate > X per minute or cross-region egress cost > configured budget.
2) Triage: run /region/capacity for impacted regions. Inspect routing.explain traces for affected requests.
3) Mitigate: temporarily increase degrade aggressiveness for noncritical routes (move gamma weight down, beta weight up in scoring) and throttle low-priority inbound traffic at edge.
4) Stabilize: create canary routing rule to pin critical routes to reserved capacity or force to local medium-chosen variants.
5) Postmortem: evaluate quality metrics for degraded responses and update per-route tolerance.

Example scenarios:
A) Short seq, latency_budget 150ms, priority high, local reserved exhausted.
- Feasible variants: medium (est p95 130ms local shared), low (p95 80ms). Score picks low only if quality budget allows; otherwise attempt spill to us-east with handshake. If spill accepted, we prefer remote medium over local low for high-priority routes where quality matters.

B) Long seq, latency_budget 1000ms, cost-sensitive route.
- Likely pick medium local (fits p95), but if local shared capacity is strained, we downgrade to low with caching/streaming-enabled to save tokens. No spill unless reserved for customer.

Open questions / trade-offs to resolve:
- How granular should tokens be? Per-variant, per-model-family, or simple per-tier? Tradeoff: granularity improves fairness but costs complexity in bookkeeping.
- How to encode quality budget in a machine-readable way? We need a simple enum (none/soft/hard) vs numeric score.
- E2E testing: we need a synthetic workload generator that mimics real seq length distributions per route to validate the heuristic. Ticket: JIRA-PLT-879.

Implementation notes / TODOs:
- Instrument routing.decision with correlation_id and emit an abbreviated trace for the console before full trace retention window (Marco).
- Add spill.offer metrics to the routing dashboard (Janelle).
- Prototype spill handshake in route-sim repo and run chaos tests with cross-region delays of 50-200ms.
- Short-term: implement scoring weights as per-route config with defaults; long-term: consider RL-based weight tuning in Optimize.

Notes to self:
- Keep the handshake minimum: a single round-trip accept/reject avoids over-allocating remote capacity.
- Prefer local quality degradation over long-haul spill for non-blocking routes.
- Make all decisions explainable: console must show why a variant was chosen (cost vs latency vs capacity).

Appendix: Sample routing decision log (human readable):
- request_id=abc123 | route=chat-completion | region=us-west1 | chosen_variant=rdx-gpt-medium-8bit | chosen_tier=shared | reason=latency_fit+capacity_best | latency_est=120ms | seq_est=64

This doc is intentionally lightweight and pragmatic. Next step: align with SRE on metrics thresholds and run a 2-week simulation with sampled traffic to quantify spill rate implications.
